title: Madame X
location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
description: A portrait of Virginie Amélie Avegno Gautreau, a Parisian socialite, rendered in a striking pose with her pale skin set against a dark background. The painting caused a scandal at the Paris Salon due to its perceived sensuality and the subject's daring décolletage. Sargent considered it his finest work, and it remains one of the most iconic portraits in American art.

title: Carnation, Lily, Lily, Rose
description: Two young girls light paper lanterns in an English garden at twilight, surrounded by carnations, lilies, and roses in full bloom. Sargent painted the scene en plein air over several months, capturing the fleeting moments of dusk each evening. The warm lantern glow against the cool fading daylight creates a magical, dreamlike atmosphere.

title: El Jaleo
description: A dramatic depiction of a Spanish Roma dancer performing with fierce intensity, her white dress swirling as musicians play behind her in the shadows. The painting captures the raw energy and passion of flamenco, with bold contrasts between the illuminated dancer and the dark, smoky background. Sargent's masterful handling of light and movement brings the nocturnal performance to vivid life.

title: The Daughters of Edward Darley Boit
description: Four young sisters are arranged in the grand foyer of their Parisian apartment, with two enormous Japanese vases flanking the composition. The youngest girl sits on the floor with a doll while her older sisters recede into progressively deeper shadow, creating a haunting sense of psychological depth. The unconventional composition and masterful treatment of interior light owe a debt to Velázquez's Las Meninas.

title: Lady Agnew of Lochnaw
description: Gertrude Vernon, Lady Agnew of Lochnaw, reclines in a bergère chair with an air of confident ease, her gaze meeting the viewer directly. The painting's virtuoso brushwork captures the luminous quality of her white dress and the subtle interplay of lavender and blue in the background silk. This portrait established Sargent's reputation as the leading portraitist of his generation.

title: Midsummer Dance
description: Couples dance around a maypole during a Swedish Midsummer celebration, their figures bathed in the soft, luminous light of the Nordic summer evening. The scene captures the joyful energy of the folk tradition with Zorn's characteristic fluid brushwork and mastery of outdoor light. The warm tones of the wooden buildings contrast with the cool greens of the surrounding landscape.

title: Self-Portrait with Model
description: Zorn depicts himself at work in his studio, palette in hand, alongside a nude model who stands behind him. The painting demonstrates his celebrated limited palette of vermilion, yellow ochre, ivory black, and lead white, achieving remarkable chromatic richness from just four pigments. The confident, bravura brushwork reveals the artist's supreme technical mastery.

title: Omnibus I
description: Passengers crowd together inside a Parisian omnibus, their faces and figures revealed by the diffuse light filtering through the windows. Zorn captures the intimacy and anonymity of modern urban life with remarkable sensitivity to reflected light and subtle tonal variations. The painting demonstrates his ability to render complex interior lighting with seemingly effortless skill.

title: The Desperate Man
description: Courbet portrays himself clutching his hair with both hands, his wide eyes staring intensely at the viewer in an expression of anguish and defiance. The dramatic self-portrait reveals the young artist's romantic temperament and bold confidence in his own talent. The tightly cropped composition and direct gaze create an unsettling psychological immediacy.

title: A Burial at Ornans
description: A monumental depiction of a funeral in Courbet's hometown of Ornans, with nearly fifty life-sized figures gathered around an open grave beneath grey Jura cliffs. The painting shocked the Parisian art world by applying the grand scale of history painting to a commonplace rural event, treating ordinary villagers with the same gravity as kings and saints. It became a manifesto of the Realist movement and a turning point in the history of art.

title: The Painter's Studio
description: Subtitled 'A Real Allegory Summing Up Seven Years of My Artistic and Moral Life,' this enormous canvas places Courbet at the center of his studio, painting a landscape while surrounded by figures representing all walks of society. A nude model and a young boy watch him work, while poets, critics, collectors, and laborers populate the shadowy edges. The painting is both a bold artistic statement and a complex social allegory of mid-nineteenth-century France.

title: The Wave
description: A massive wave crests and curls against a stormy sky on the Normandy coast, painted with thick, tactile impasto that gives the water an almost sculptural presence. Courbet applied paint with palette knives to capture the raw, elemental force of the sea. The painting is one of a series of seascapes that profoundly influenced later artists, from Whistler to the Abstract Expressionists.

title: The Gleaners
description: Three peasant women bend low to gather the last stalks of wheat left behind after the harvest, their stooped forms silhouetted against a golden field stretching to the horizon. Millet elevates this humble act of survival into a composition of monumental dignity, drawing on classical and biblical traditions. The painting was controversial for its sympathetic portrayal of rural poverty and remains an enduring symbol of agrarian labor.

title: The Sower
description: A solitary peasant strides down a hillside at dusk, scattering seed with a powerful, sweeping gesture that fills the composition with dynamic energy. Millet transforms the humble act of sowing into a heroic image, the figure's dark silhouette dominating the twilight landscape. The painting deeply influenced Van Gogh, who made multiple copies of it throughout his career.

title: Barge Haulers on the Volga
description: Eleven men strain against their harnesses as they drag a barge along the sandy banks of the Volga River under a blazing summer sky. Repin individualizes each hauler with remarkable psychological depth, from the resigned elder to the defiant youth who straightens against his strap. The painting became an icon of Russian social realism and a powerful indictment of human exploitation.

title: Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks
description: A boisterous group of Zaporozhian Cossacks crowd around a scribe, roaring with laughter as they dictate a mocking reply to the Ottoman Sultan's demand for their surrender. Repin spent over a decade on the painting, meticulously researching costumes and gathering individual character studies. The result is a riotous celebration of defiant humor and collective camaraderie.

title: Ivan the Terrible and His Son Ivan
description: Tsar Ivan IV clutches his dying son in horror, blood seeping through his fingers as he realizes the fatal blow he has struck in a fit of rage. The painting captures the precise moment of agonized recognition, Ivan's wide, terrified eyes contrasting with his son's fading gaze of forgiveness. Repin's masterwork is one of the most psychologically devastating paintings in all of Russian art.
